rationale: In the theoretical framing, the citing paper uses Yang et al. (2018), alongside related process-tracing work, as evidence for a core premise: respondents' information processing changes across repeated elicitation questions. It also cites Yang et al. (2018) specifically in the discussion to support the claim that incentive alignment alone does not ensure a perfect match with real-world behavior. While one occurrence is in a grouped literature list, the citation is tied to key arguments that motivate hypotheses and interpret results. This makes the citation function meaningfully substantive rather than a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper treats the cited paper as prior empirical groundwork for its adaptation and external-validity argument. It relies on that groundwork to motivate and contextualize its own tests of how additional elicitation questions can reduce external validity.
